stories.headline_working: A digital twin of Alaska permafrost, built on 3 years of buried fiber-optic data

claims.text: A Penn State-led team buried a pair of 1-kilometer-long fiber-optic cables to collect thermal and seismic data from the ground.
claims.source_title: Digital twin of Alaska permafrost yields real-time forecasts (Phys.org)
claims.verification: Verbatim: the team buried a pair of 1-kilometer-long (0.6-mile-long) fiber-optic cables capable of collecting thermal and seismic data from the ground.

claims.text: A section of the cables runs along a road embankment in Utqiagvik, Alaska, the northernmost city in the United States.
claims.verification: Verbatim: A short section of these cables runs along a road embankment; a specific road embankment in Utqiagvik, Alaska the northernmost city in the United States.

claims.text: The cables collected temperature and seismic data from September 2021 to June 2024.
claims.verification: Verbatim: collecting temperature and seismic data from September 2021 to June 2024.

claims.text: Permafrost worldwide is warming, with ground temperatures increasing by up to almost 2 degrees F per decade (a general statement about global permafrost, not the measured Utqiagvik embankment).
claims.value: up to almost 2 F per decade (global)
claims.verification: Verbatim article narration: ground temperatures increasing by up to almost 2 F per decade. NOTE: describes global permafrost, not the site's own readings; hedge accordingly.

claims.text: Lead researcher Ming Xiao said about 10 more years of data would be extremely valuable to the broader scientific community.
claims.verification: Verbatim: If we have, say, 10 more years of data, that would be extremely valuable. Aspiration, not a funded plan.

claims.text: The work was led by Ming Xiao (professor of civil engineering) and Tieyuan Zhu (associate professor of geosciences), both at Penn State.
claims.value: Ming Xiao, Tieyuan Zhu, Penn State
claims.verification: Article names Ming Xiao (civil engineering) and Tieyuan Zhu (geosciences) at Penn State University.

claims.text: The study was published in the Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface in 2026.
claims.verification: Phys.org cites Journal of Geophysical Research: Earth Surface (2026). Primary paper DOI 10.1029/2025JF008787 could not be independently fetched (Wiley 402); journal/year rests on Phys.org attribution only.

claims.text: The framework is a digital twin: it processes terabytes of data to create a real-time simulation of an area or object.
claims.verification: Verbatim: This framework is known as a digital twin, which processes terabytes of data to create an extremely accurate, real-time simulation of an area or object.

claims.text: The method combines physics equations, mathematical functions, and AI-powered machine learning (physics-informed machine learning).
claims.verification: Article uses physics-informed and describes complex equations, mathematical functions and AI-powered machine learning. HONESTY NOTE: narrow applied ML fused with physics, not generative or general AI.

claims.text: The approach more accurately predicts the embankment permafrost's physical properties, including unfrozen water content, ground temperature, and how heat moves through the ground.
claims.value: unfrozen water content, ground temperature, heat flow
claims.verification: Verbatim: more accurately predict detailed physical properties of the embankment's permafrost, including unfrozen water content, changes to ground temperature and how heat moved through the ground.

claims.text: The digital twin simulates one specific road embankment, but the researchers say the idea and process can be broadly transferable to other cold-region infrastructure monitoring.
claims.verification: Verbatim: Although the digital twin simulates a specific road embankment, Xiao said the idea and process can be broadly transferable to other infrastructure monitoring in cold regions. HONESTY FIREWALL: one prototype embankment, not a statewide deployment.

claims.text: Arctic communities and governments could see billions of dollars in infrastructure damage over the coming decades if the thawing trend continues.
claims.verification: Verbatim: Arctic communities and governments could see billions of dollars in infrastructure damage over the coming decades if this trend continues. Projection, hedge as could see.

stories.single_source_note: Every permafrost fact traces to one fetched source, the Phys.org press article (Penn State/AGU sourced), 2026-06-16. The peer-reviewed paper (Wiley JGR) was paywalled (HTTP 402). Attribute clearly throughout ('according to Penn State / the study'). Frame as recent research (~5 weeks old), not breaking news.

dropped.claim: Primary paper contents from Wiley JGR DOI 10.1029/2025JF008787
dropped.reason: HTTP 402 paywall; could not independently confirm. All facts trace to Phys.org.

dropped.claim: Cables will stay in the ground ~10 more years as a firm plan
dropped.reason: Source supports only an aspirational statement; softened into PERMA-05.

stories.headline_working: 500-plus comments, fewer than a dozen in favor: the North Slope AI data-center lease

claims.text: Alaska DNR proposes to lease a 715.4-acre parcel of North Slope state land (ADL 422741) for a natural-gas-powered high-performance-computing facility.
claims.verification: Verbatim: 715.4 acre parcel and ADL 422741; use described as modular high-performance computing units, on-site natural gas power generation.

claims.text: The proposed lease term is 50 years.
claims.verification: DNR notice states 50-year lease; Northern.org states 50-year negotiated lease.

claims.text: The public comment deadline was July 17, 2026, at 4:30 PM Alaska Daylight Time.
claims.verification: DNR notice: July 17, 2026 at 4:30 PM Alaska Daylight Time; ADN confirms deadline extended to July 17.

claims.text: The site is about 26 miles south of Deadhorse and roughly one mile west of the Dalton Highway.
claims.value: ~26 mi S of Deadhorse, ~1 mi W of Dalton Hwy
claims.verification: DNR notice: adjacent to the Dalton Highway approximately 26 miles south of Deadhorse; Northern.org: about 26 miles south of Deadhorse and roughly one mile west of the Dalton Highway.

claims.text: DNR received more than 500 public comments, of which fewer than a dozen endorsed the project.
claims.source_title: Data center critics flood Alaska land managers (ADN / Northern Journal)
claims.verification: Verbatim: More than 500 public comments and Fewer than a dozen comments endorsed the project.

claims.text: The comment period was extended to July 17 due to the volume of comments, public interest, and requests for extension.
claims.source_title: Data center critics flood Alaska land managers (ADN)
claims.verification: Verbatim: extended due to the volume of comments received, public interest and requests for extension.

claims.text: The campus would include 1 to 3 gigawatts of on-site natural-gas-fired generation.
claims.source_title: STAK Energy campus lease-doc analysis (Northern Journal)
claims.verification: Verbatim: 1 to 3 gigawatts of on-site natural gas-fired generation. ADN corroborates a gigawatt or more.

claims.text: The facility would burn roughly 350 to 500 million standard cubic feet of gas per day.
claims.source_title: STAK Energy campus lease-doc analysis (Northern Journal)
claims.verification: Verbatim: roughly 350 to 500 million standard cubic feet of gas per day.

claims.text: Construction would require roughly 7.1 million cubic yards of gravel fill.
claims.source_title: STAK Energy campus lease-doc analysis (Northern Journal)
claims.verification: Verbatim: roughly 7.1 million cubic yards of gravel fill.

claims.text: Estimated project cost exceeds $10 billion, with roughly $500 million in up-front site development.
claims.source_title: Data center critics flood Alaska land managers (ADN); Northern Journal
claims.verification: ADN: more than $10 billion; Northern.org: approximately $500 million in site evaluation, engineering, gravel road and pad construction.

dropped.reason: Not stated on the DNR notice, Northern.org, or ADN pages fetched. Unverified.

stories.headline_working: Counting 100,000-plus caribou in gigapixel aerial mosaics with a two-stage detector

claims.text: A two-stage deep-learning pipeline pairs a patch-based pretrain classifier with HerdNet point detection to count caribou in gigapixel aerial mosaics.
claims.source_title: Caribou detection in aerial imagery (Frontiers in Ecology and Evolution)
claims.verification: Article: patch-based pretrain network (PPN) followed by HerdNet, a point-based detection network.

claims.text: The method achieved F1 scores of 95.5% (2017) and 93.3% (2019), beating baseline initialization.
claims.verification: Verbatim: F1: 95.5%/93.3% vs. 91.5%/90.4% (proposed vs baseline, 2017/2019).

claims.text: The study covers five caribou herds distributed across Alaska (Central Arctic, Fortymile, Porcupine, Teshekpuk, Western Arctic).
claims.verification: Article names CAH, FCH, PCH, TCH, WAH, five caribou herds distributed across Alaska.

claims.text: Imagery was captured at roughly 2 to 4.3 cm ground-sampling distance from about 450 m altitude during July 2017 and July 2019 surveys.
claims.verification: Verbatim: GSD values ranging from 2 to 4.3 cm; flight altitude was approximately 450 meters; July 2017 and July 2019.

claims.text: The study was published in Frontiers in Ecology and Evolution on February 26, 2026.
claims.verification: Metadata: Frontiers in Ecology and Evolution, published 26 February 2026.

dropped.reason: Exact figure could not be confirmed; use 'more than 100,000 across five herds' only, re-verify before publishing.

notes: PRIMARY (permafrost-digital-twin) clears the bar: 12 verified claims (9 high-confidence), enough for an 8-9 slide deck. Single-source caveat (Phys.org; Wiley paywalled) requires clear attribution. Honesty firewall PERMA-11 and the narrow-ML note PERMA-09 MUST appear. STAK fallback rock-solid (10 claims). Caribou fallback usable (5 claims), avoid a precise total.
